data.title: 3 underrated ski resorts in Canada, Japan and Italy to visit in winter 2026
data.description:
A ski trip is a wonderful opportunity to spend time with loved ones, hit the slopes, engage in wellness activities and practise an active hobby or two.
Around the globe, there are spectacular destinations well known for skiing, including several in western Europe and Japan, but there are also a few underrated gems. Here are three recommendations.
Courmayeur, Italy, and beyond
The Dolomites are part of the Alps in Italy and are listed as a Unesco World Heritage site for outstanding universal value, from geography to wildlife and human history.
Northern Italy has been getting a fair bit of buzz recently, since it will be hosting the Winter Olympics and Paralympics in Milan and Cortina in February.

There are plenty of ski destinations to love near this adventurous region of Italy, and one of them is Courmayeur. The region has a long-standing tradition of winter sports activities and is celebrating the 90th anniversary of its Monte Bianco Ski & Snowboard School.
The region, located near the French border, allows access to Mont Blanc and offers some amazing places to ski for skiers of any level. Courmayeur is also a stylish place, known for high-end dining.
One great hotel option is the Grand Hotel Courmayeur, which has dramatic views of Mont Blanc and is easily accessible from Turin, Milan and Geneva.
After a long day spent on the slopes, guests can recover in the Erre Spa with its indoor heated pool, sauna, Turkish hammam, sensory showers and more. Even better, the five-star property matches many travel budgets, with rates starting at less than US$300 a night.
Culture lovers will enjoy visiting the region of South Tyrol, a decidedly German part of Italy. Travellers might lose track of where they are, as its jagged mountain peaks, bucolic farms and charming villages often look more like something from Switzerland or Austria than Italy, but this multicultural gem is also home to some amazing ski resorts.
Hokkaido, Niigata and Nagano in Japan

Hokkaido, the northernmost of Japan’s major islands, is well known for its ski areas, and it is not hard to see why: besides having the mountains that are required for skiing, it also gets a ton of snow.
Sapporo, the island’s largest city, gets about 130 days of snowfall a year, contributing to an annual total of more than 19 feet (5.8 metres).
The flight from Tokyo to Sapporo is just over 90 minutes, making Hokkaido a convenient location to enjoy some incredible skiing, snowboarding and winter travel experiences, whether you are a diehard black diamond skier or want to integrate some active wellness experiences into your trip.
Not too far from Sapporo is Niseko Village, home to several properties that can become a great base camp for a ski trip and often cost about 30 per cent less than most ski resorts in Europe or the United States.
Known for its dry, fluffy powder snow called “Japow”, the region is a wonderful place to ski, with its Niseko Village Snow School, opportunities for guided runs and more.

“Beyond the snow, Niseko has transformed from a once-sleepy ski town into an international melting pot,” says Stephanie Chai, founder and CEO of the travel website The Luxe Nomad. “When I’m there in winter, I can walk on the street or sit at a bar and count at least 10 different languages from visitors from all over the world.
“The cuisine is also phenomenal: not only are there top Japanese restaurants, but internationally acclaimed ones as well, and world-class accommodation from ski-in, ski-out condo hotels to luxury chalets. Not to mention Japanese onsen culture – the perfect way to end a day up in the mountains.”
If you are visiting Japan during the winter and do not need to visit the most popular ski destination in the country, where would you go?
There are two special places: Niigata prefecture and Nagano prefecture. Located not too far from Tokyo, both are easily accessible and provide great skiing and active winter experiences.
Nagano is home to what is called the “Japanese Alps”, and is known as the place where Japanese macaques, or snow monkeys, warm up during winter in natural hot springs at the Jigokudani Monkey Park.


Beyond that adorable natural wonder, Nagano offers Japow snow, hot-spring villages, historical temples and international-friendly tourism infrastructure, including ski schools.
Niigata, located just two hours away from Tokyo by bullet train, is another wonderful ski resort destination. The region’s snowfall comes from the wind blowing from the Sea of Japan against the Echigo Mountains, creating a snowier climate than other cities of the same latitude.
This year is particularly special for the prefecture, as 2026 marks the 115th anniversary of skiing being introduced in the mountains of Echigo.
Niigata is also home to respected cultural practices, such as the 1,200-year-old Echigo Jofu, a Unesco-listed textile-making tradition that uses snow to refine hemp fabric, which is then made into summer kimonos. Niigata also has the highest number of sake breweries in Japan, in large part due to its significant rice production.
“Niigata prefecture is one of Japan’s leading ski resorts, highly accessible from Tokyo Station by Shinkansen in as little as 70 minutes [to Echigo-Yuzawa],” says Kenichi Shirai, executive vice-president of the Niigata Prefectural Tourism Association. “The Yuzawa and Myoko areas, in particular, have a high concentration of ski resorts and their appeal lies in the combination of abundant natural snow and well-equipped surrounding areas with hot spring towns and resort hotels.”
Quebec, Canada

Quebec might be best known for its French-Canadian culture and Unesco-listed Old Quebec City, but venture further afield and you will find ample ski resorts and opportunities to hit fresh powder.
Travellers can find plenty to do, see and love in ski towns such as Mont-Tremblant – named for the way the mountain sometimes trembles with geological activity – and Charlevoix, located between the beautiful Laurentides Wildlife Reserve and the scenic St Lawrence River.
Le Massif de Charlevoix offers amazing skiing east of the Rocky Mountains. Skiers can enjoy 53 runs of all levels as well as 98 acres (40 hectares) of off-piste skiing.
This Canadian winter wonderland enjoys a stunning 254 inches of snow annually, which is over 21 feet or 6.4 metres.
One recommendation for staying in Quebec is the Club Med Quebec Charlevoix, the brand’s first four-season mountain resort and the first Club Med in Canada, offering an easy all-inclusive ski resort option.
Travellers can easily rent their gear, take lessons for all levels, employ a guide and enjoy sledding with the children at the mountain’s 7.5km (4.7-mile) sledding track.
Located an hour from Quebec City, it is a great option for those who want to combine skiing with an exploration of the charming and historical Quebec City, which is its own brand of winter wonderland.

data.title: India overtakes Japan, Bali mulls bank check requirement: 7 Asia highlights
data.description:
We have selected seven stories from the SCMP’s coverage of Asia over the past week that resonated with our readers and shed light on topical issues. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. India overtakes Japan in economic size – while prosperity trails far behind
India ushered in the new year with a symbolic triumph as its economy edged past Japan’s to become the world’s fourth largest by nominal gross domestic product.
2. Nearly 94% of Malaysians who renounced citizenship chose Singapore, data shows

Most Malaysians who renounced their citizenship over the past five years went on to become Singaporeans, official data shows, underscoring the city state’s pull amid long-standing brain drain concerns.
3. The Maduro effect: US raid accelerates Asia’s drift away from Washington
The United States’ dramatic abduction of Venezuela’s former leader Nicolas Maduro at the weekend has sent shock waves across Asia, where analysts say authoritarian rulers, already deeply wary of Washington’s erratic behaviour, are likely to draw closer to Moscow and Beijing.
4. Bali’s toughest tourism proposal yet: checking foreign visitors’ bank accounts

Indonesia’s Bali is weighing a regulation that would require foreign tourists to disclose how much money they have had in their bank accounts over the past three months, the island’s most far-reaching proposal yet in a drive to deter low-spending visitors and promote what officials call “quality tourism”.
5. Could Singapore remove Pritam Singh as Leader of the Opposition?
In an unprecedented move in Singapore, Workers’ Party (WP) chief Pritam Singh could be stripped of his title as Leader of Opposition and lose his privileges when Singapore’s parliamentarians debate his suitability for the role next week, legal experts say.
6. Soap opera in the sky: why Philippine Airlines’ safety video became a viral hit

Philippine Airlines has drawn widespread praise online for a new in-flight safety video that turns routine cabin instructions into a full-blown Filipino soap opera.
7. Is 12 too young? Rare matricide case tests Indonesia’s juvenile justice system
A rare case of matricide in Indonesia in which a girl, 12, was arrested for the murder of her mother has put the country’s treatment of juvenile suspects in the spotlight.

data.title: Standard Chartered Taipei Charity Marathon hands out finishers’ medals with glaring typo
data.description:
Ahead of the Standard Chartered Hong Kong Marathon this weekend, attention turned to its sister event, the Standard Chartered Taipei Charity Marathon, after 26,000 finishers spotted a glaring typo on their medals on Sunday.
The coveted medals for finishers spelled “marathon” as “marathan,” sparking derision and discussions online.
“My first full marathon came with a limited-edition souvenir,” one runner quipped.
Others joked that the error meant that the medals were “turning into a collector’s item immediately”.
When asked by the Post about medal checks for this Sunday’s Hong Kong marathon, the race organiser offered a curt reply: “We do not comment on other races.”

Chinese Taipei Road Running Association, the Taipei Charity Marathon organisers, announced on Monday that they would reprint medals without errors and mail them free of charge to all affected finishers.
“Due to a production oversight, the text on this year’s medals contained errors,” said the statement. “We sincerely apologise to all runners impacted and are remaking correct versions for free replacement.”
The affected runners need to register via a link by January 18.
The charity marathon attracted 26,000 participants across the full marathon, half marathon, 11km and 3km distances.
Hong Weiqi claimed the men’s full marathon title in 2 hours, 30 minutes and 11 seconds, while Japan’s Kana Numata dominated the women’s race in 2:40:15.

data.title: South Korea’s worst air disaster: fury erupts over ‘mound of death’
data.description:
South Korea has belatedly acknowledged that a concrete structure built beyond a runway turned a routine emergency landing into the deadliest-ever domestic aviation disaster, in a reversal that has unleashed a furious debate over safety oversight and accountability.
Newly disclosed findings based on computer simulations show the structure – originally constructed to support navigation equipment at Muan International Airport – acted as a deadly barrier. Without it, investigators concluded that all 181 passengers and crew would likely have survived the crash on December 29, 2024.
Instead, Jeju Air Flight 2216 slammed into the solid concrete base about 250 metres (820 feet) beyond the runway’s end, erupting into flames and killing 179 of the people on board. The aircraft had attempted a wheels-up landing after a suspected bird strike damaged its undercarriage, investigators found.
The transport ministry’s Aviation and Railway Accident Investigation Board concluded that without the embankment, which contained 19 buried concrete columns installed in 2007 to prop up a guidance antenna, the aircraft would likely have slid a few hundred metres before grinding to a halt.

Officials had covered the structure with soil and turf to meet rules requiring soft terrain, but the hardened base breached international standards that since 2010 have required such facilities to use “frangible” – or breakable – materials designed to yield on impact.
Opposition lawmakers have accused the government of negligence and an attempted cover-up, alleging the findings were completed months before their public release.
People Power Party (PPP) legislator Kim Eun-hye and six others, who revealed the findings of the report on Friday, have called for a parliamentary inquiry, demanding accountability for officials who authorised or oversaw the design of the “mound of death” and failed to replace it during runway upgrades completed just 10 months before the crash.
Of the 44 people investigated in connection with the crash, none were high-ranking officials, the lawmakers claimed.


Kim demanded that “the truth must be uncovered” as to how the localiser structure “became a deadly obstacle”.
“Investigations must be expanded comprehensively, from flawed design to poor improvement work,” she told reporters on Friday.
Aviation experts warn that political mudslinging could delay essential fixes at airports nationwide.
Similar non-frangible installations remain at several sites, including Jeju Airport, where localiser antennas are mounted on steel frames “that even a tank wouldn’t be able to break through”, Kim Kwang-il, a professor of aeronautics at Silla University, told This Week in Asia.
“A similar accident could happen today or tomorrow,” he warned. “We don’t need political bickering.”
A similar accident could happen today or tomorrow
Kim Kwang-il, aviation expert
Budget constraints and bureaucratic inertia have delayed upgrades – and although the transport ministry says five of nine sites have been addressed, replacement work at Jeju will not finish until March 2027. Muan Airport remains closed.
“This is a huge amount of concrete, you could construct a building on it,” Park Young-song, an international airport designer, said about the localiser at Muan to JTBC news last month. “I really don’t understand why they built it like this.”
The transport ministry now concedes that the embankment should have been replaced with breakable materials during earlier renovations. Safety standards for such equipment had been in force for more than a decade when that project began.
But critics argue that the pace – and the ministry’s dual role as both regulator and investigator – erodes public trust. Lawmakers from both parties have since agreed to form an independent accident investigation agency, though its launch could take months.
“Politicising safety issues is the last thing we need,” Professor Lee Gun-young of the Korea National University of Transportation told This Week in Asia.
“The priority should be a fair, thorough investigation and concrete measures to prevent a recurrence.”

data.title: More cross-border tours, visitors expected on expanded Hong Kong high-speed rail
data.description:
More train tour packages to mainland China are expected to be launched with 16 newly added destinations to Hong Kong’s high-speed rail network, industry leaders have said, with the enhanced connectivity expected to boost cross-border traveller numbers in both directions.
Timothy Chui Ting-pong, executive director of the Hong Kong Tourism Association, said on Monday that local travel agencies were pivoting to capitalise on this “game-changing” high-speed rail network as a formidable competitor to air travel for mid-range destinations, including cities such as Nanjing.
“We will be converting itineraries that were originally flight-based into high-speed rail products,” Chui told a radio programme.
“While a flight to Nanjing takes about two and a half hours, once you factor in the time spent travelling to the airport and arriving early for check-in, the total journey can take seven to eight hours. That is roughly the same as the seven-and-a-half-hour direct high-speed rail journey, making the train very competitive.”
The MTR Corporation announced on Sunday that 16 new destinations would be added to the Hong Kong section of the high-speed rail system starting on January 26, bringing the total number of mainland stations with direct access to 110.
The expansion connects the city with major hubs in eastern China, including Nanjing, Wuxi, and Hefei, alongside 13 other stations across Guangdong, Fujian and Hunan provinces, such as Qingyuan, Shantou South and Fuzhou South.
Chui noted that the January 26 launch was strategically timed to meet the peak travel demand of the coming Lunar New Year, with the expanded network serving as a “new growth engine” for the tourism sector on both sides of the border throughout the nine-day holiday from February 15 to 23 in mainland China.
He further explained how the new stops in Hefei and Wuxi would create a “Jiangnan travel loop”, referring to the region south of the Yangtze River, with agencies designing routes featuring visits to cultural landmarks as well as the cosmopolitan hub of Nanjing.
“From Hefei, you can take in the sights through Wuxi and Lake Tai, ending in Nanjing to experience the big city and enjoy good food before returning directly to Hong Kong. This kind of ‘one-stop’ itinerary is now entirely possible,” Chui said.
Beyond leisure, Chui identified Nanjing as a major centre for hi-tech development in robotics, making the direct rail link useful for facilitating student tours and business exchanges.

Mainland travel agencies were also moving quickly, he noted, designing “all-in-one” rail products that would bring travellers directly from cities such as Nanjing and Wuxi to Hong Kong, and bundling the trips with excursions to Macau.
As part of the network expansion, the high-speed sleeper train to Shanghai Hongqiao station will also be upgraded from a weekend-only to a daily service, while the frequency of round trips to Guangzhou South will increase to 24 per day to better serve those living within an hour’s journey of the Greater Bay Area.
“While Hong Kong travellers are often characterised as being in a rush and preferring flights, the overnight sleeper service offers a unique value proposition, particularly for families and friends from the mainland,” Chui said.
“Mainland residents are very accustomed to these sleeper trains. In a cabin with four beds, you can have a family of four or a group of close friends travelling together, departing after work, sleeping on the train and arriving in Hong Kong by the next morning to start their holiday immediately.”
Fanny Yeung Shuk-fun, executive director of the Travel Industry Council, said itinerary planning would become simpler with the enhanced sleeper train services.
“Previously, if travellers wanted to visit Shanghai and then head to other nearby areas before returning, it was very difficult to coordinate,” she said.
Yeung noted that high-speed rail fares were generally more stable than airfares, and would help travel agencies manage costs more effectively during peak seasons and offer more competitive tour packages to attract mainland tourists.
Lawmaker Gary Chan Hak-kan said the expansion of the high-speed rail network would provide Hong Kong residents with more flexible travel options to the mainland and easier access to cultural and scenic landmarks in cities such as Nanjing and Wuxi.
“It also offers mainland residents a more convenient direct route to Hong Kong for business and tourism, allowing them to experience the city’s distinctive fusion of Chinese and Western influences,” he added.

data.title: China planning dozens of plants that use coal instead of oil to make plastics
data.description:
China is stepping up its efforts to use its vast coal reserves instead of oil to produce plastics and synthetic rubber.
Coal can be refined to make olefins – an essential raw material for tens of thousands of chemical products that include plastics, synthetic fibres and rubber.
While the mainstream practice is to produce olefins from oil, using coal can bring significant cost savings and might help to reduce the country’s reliance on imported fuel at a time when Donald Trump is seeking to expand US control over the world’s oil supplies.
In recent years, Beijing has approved a total of 36 projects – 20 of which are already operating – with a total annual capacity exceeding 24 million tonnes in coal-rich areas such as Shaanxi and Inner Mongolia, China Science Daily reported.
The process will remain profitable so long as the oil price stays above US$35 a barrel, according to calculations by industry blogger PTGChemical.
The blog said that producing materials from coal cost around 6,300 yuan (US$900) per tonne – about 1,500 yuan less than using oil.
International oil prices have dropped from over US$120 per barrel in March 2022 to less than US$60.
The technology behind this project comes from the Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics in a process where coal is used to produce methanol, before it is then converted into olefins.
The report said that researchers had been using AI to speed up the development process, by modelling the way factories would operate.
A single plant can process 3.6 million tonnes of methanol per year to produce more than 1 million tonnes of olefins.
“[This] technology has led and supported the rapid development of China’s coal-to-olefins industry. It plays a significant role in alleviating oil supply shortages and ensuring energy security and the stable operation of the petrochemical industry chain,” Liu Zhongmin, a member of the Chinese Academy of Engineering and director of the Dalian institute, said.
“Compared with traditional coal use, such as combustion, [the technology] reduces carbon dioxide emissions by about half,” he said.
The institute began researching coal-to-olefins technology in the 1980s, but the technology needed substantial government funding to make it a viable proposition.
When the first generation of the relevant technology was ready in 1997, international oil prices plummeted to around US$10 per barrel, making the process too costly.
It was not until oil prices recovered in 2004 that the technology regained momentum. Backed by state-owned enterprises, industrialisation efforts resumed in Shaanxi, a province rich in coal resources.
In 2010, the world’s first coal-to-olefins plant opened in Baotou in Inner Mongolia.
Ordos, another coal-rich city in the region, now hosts the world’s largest single-site coal-to-olefins plant, which began operations last April.
In 2023, China consumed 68 million tonnes of ethylene, one of the main by-products of oil and a key source material for plastics and other products.
T

data.title: Coleman Wong beats Ugo Blanchet to advance to second round of qualifying at Australian Open
data.description:
Hong Kong’s top tennis player Coleman Wong Chak-lam advanced to the next round of the Australian Open qualifying after defeating France’s world No 142 Ugo Blanchet 6-3, 7-6 in the first round of qualifying on Tuesday.
Wong needed only 35 minutes to win the first set after converting his break point opportunities and putting the qualifying No 31 seed Blanchet on the defensive at Melbourne Park.
The second set was more competitive, with both players holding serve to take the set to a tiebreaker. The tiebreak was a tight affair until Wong forced Blanchet into a backhand error, sealing the match for the Hongkonger.
Wong hit 33 winners, served 14 aces and committed only 18 unforced errors in a match that lasted 90 minutes.
After reaching the quarter-finals at the Bank of China Hong Kong Tennis Open 2026 last week, Wong climbed 18 places in the ATP world rankings to No 132, close to his career-high of No 128.
The 21-year-old reached the US Open main draw from qualifying last year and became the first Hongkonger to make the round of 32 in a grand slam men’s singles event, losing an enthralling five-set tussle with Andrey Rublev.
Last week’s Hong Kong Open also made him the first local player to reach the quarter-finals of an ATP Tour event.
Wong will play Pablo Llamas Ruiz in the second round of qualifying after the Spaniard beat Henry Bernet of Switzerland 3-6, 6-3, 7-6.

data.title: Singapore jails helper who slapped, hit and kicked elderly dementia patient
data.description:
A domestic helper in Singapore abused a 90-year-old woman she was supposed to care for, including slapping her face and hitting her with a diaper.
Some of the mistreatment occurred while the victim, who has moderate vascular dementia, was partially unclothed, as her helper, Nwe Nwe San, attempted to help her put on a diaper.
Nwe Nwe San, a 49-year-old Myanmar national, was jailed for 52 weeks on Monday.
She pleaded guilty through an interpreter to two counts of voluntarily causing hurt to a vulnerable person.
Her abuse was captured on closed-circuit television (CCTV) footage, which was played in court. At times, the frail victim could be seen raising her hands to defend herself against Nwe Nwe San, who stood before her.
In addition to dementia, the victim is minimally communicative, requires help to move around and wears a diaper.
Nwe Nwe San was engaged by the victim’s 68-year-old son in 2017 to care for his mother.
He discovered the abuse when he randomly checked CCTV footage while visiting his mother on July 9, 2025.
When the victim was taken to hospital for a urinary infection on July 30, 2025, doctors informed her son that she had bruises on her body.
She was also found to have several rib fractures, although doctors could not pinpoint when they occurred. The son reviewed CCTV footage again and lodged a police report on August 2, 2025.
On July 16, 2025, Nwe Nwe San kicked the victim’s knee, prodded her head twice, then grabbed her by the shirt and pushed her onto a sofa, according to court documents.
At various points, she grabbed the victim’s hair, slapped her face at least two times and struck her arm with a diaper.
On July 28, 2025, while attempting to put a diaper on the victim, the helper grabbed the victim’s right leg and pushed it towards her while the elderly woman was naked from the waist down.
Nwe Nwe San grabbed the victim by her shirt, swung her around several times and repeatedly pushed her onto the sofa. The mistreatment caused the victim to hit her head on the sofa. She also inserted her finger into the victim’s mouth.
At one point, the victim gestured towards the toilet but was ignored. She then pressed her hands together in a pleading gesture towards Nwe Nwe San.
According to court documents, Nwe Nwe San admitted to manhandling the victim as she was frustrated at having to care for the woman on her own.
The victim’s son checked in every weekend. After learning about the helper’s frustrations, he arranged for a part-time helper to assist her.
The prosecution sought 13 to 15 months’ jail, describing the victim as a “highly vulnerable nonagenarian”.
“We would also like to highlight that while the doctor could not pinpoint exactly when the fractures were sustained, it is clear that the nature of the abuse likely resulted in the fractures,” Deputy Public Prosecutor Caleb Looi said.
Nwe Nwe San, who was unrepresented, told the court that she had committed the acts in an attempt to bring the victim’s condition to her family’s attention. This was dismissed by Looi as an afterthought.
He noted that there were better ways to raise concerns and that the victim’s son had in fact arranged for part-time help.
District Judge Lorraine Ho said that while caring for an ailing senior can be challenging, abusing the victim was not a way to resolve issues, manage frustration or gain the family’s attention.
It was not for the accused to take matters into her own hands by deliberately hurting someone, Judge Ho added.
For voluntarily causing hurt to a vulnerable person, Nwe Nwe San could have been jailed for up to six years, fined up to S$10,000 (US$7,800), or both.
This story was first published by CNA

data.title: Hong Kong airport has recovered well from the pandemic
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
I refer to the article, “HK airport sees decline in connectivity after pandemic” (January 5), which showed findings and figures from three years ago. It would be useful for us to share the latest status of connectivity at Hong Kong International Airport.
Since 2023, HKIA has seen a robust recovery and growth in traffic volume and destination network. The progress in 2025 was particularly noteworthy, with 30 global destinations added to our network. HKIA is currently connected to about 220 destinations around the world and the Chinese mainland. Some 140 airlines operate in Hong Kong, exceeding the pre-pandemic level. Our network of destinations compares favourably to most major airports in the region.
But competition remains very intense and we remain vigilant in our efforts to further enhance our competitiveness.
We expect to release consolidated air traffic figures in 2025 soon. Passenger traffic is expected to exceed 61 million trips. As we raced towards the finishing line last year, we were boosted by a strong Christmas peak, during which traffic surpassed 200,000 passenger trips on eight days.
On the other hand, in 2025 HKIA was once again crowned the world’s busiest cargo airport, the 14th time since 2010. We are one of the few major airports in the world busy with both passenger and cargo traffic.
We expect our air traffic and network will continue to grow. Airport Authority Hong Kong has been proactively supporting the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region government in expanding bilateral air services, and significant progress has been made in the past few years.
Our competitive advantages are derived from different factors, not the least our close connections to the vast market in the Chinese Mainland. We serve the Greater Bay Area as our home market. An intricate and efficient network brings passengers from the rest of the Greater Bay Area to HKIA for international travel. We relentlessly enhance our sea- and land-based intermodal services for the Greater Bay Area. The latest addition is the “Park and Fly” service that enables passengers from Guangdong province to drive to our airport and fly to international destinations.
In parallel, we will continue to enhance traffic between different mainland cities and Hong Kong and connect them with the rest of the world.
HKIA will remain steadfast in fulfilling our mission as a leading international aviation hub in Asia.
Chapman Fong, director, hub development, Airport Authority Hong Kong
Medical fee hike hits the middle class harder
Although raising medical fees may indeed help reduce overcrowding and shorten wait times at public hospitals, the measure overlooks middle-class families. Hongkongers who can afford to pay more may turn to private care, while those who are eligible for a waiver will apply for one. But the middle class, sandwiched in between, is left to bear the brunt of the fee increases.
Furthermore, the lack of 24-hour clinics in Hong Kong exacerbates the problem. Hongkongers who need medical attention outside regular clinic hours have little choice but to turn to emergency services at public hospitals.
For hospital operations to be truly sustainable, we must do more than adjust fees. There must be a joint effort to expand overnight outpatient services in both the public and private sectors. By increasing the availability of late-night medical services, more patients can be diverted from overcrowded public hospitals to other facilities, creating a more balanced and responsive healthcare system.
Karen Cheung, Sha Tin
High-speed rail link is a resounding success
I refer to “Passengers made 30 million trips in 2025 with Hong Kong’s high-speed rail line” (January 4). This would be the definition of “very well accepted”, as 30 million is four times the local population.
But nobody could forget the local opposition to this project – the very loud voices opposed to mainland Chinese officers working at the station in Hong Kong.
I’m a frequent user of this enormously efficient facility and I remember the days when a trip from Hong Kong to Guangzhou took hours. Now I’m not out for revenge and would not suggest banning those well-meaning opponents from using the facility, but I also would not object to charging them double.
Roland Guettler, Lai Chi Kok

data.title: Private art museum Dib Bangkok opens in Thailand in honour of late collector
data.description:
With his curly hair and thick-rimmed glasses, the late Thai collector Petch Osathanugrah cut a distinct figure. An industrialist, musician and heir to a popular energy drink company, he amassed a vast collection of more than 1,000 artworks and was in the early stages of building a private museum in Bangkok when he died from a heart attack in 2023.
In December, his son Purat “Chang” Osathanugrah finally opened Dib Bangkok. The museum is housed in a 1980s steel warehouse beautifully converted by US-based Thai architect Kulapat Yantrasast.
“My father had been talking about building a museum for the better part of my life, since before I could walk. What I inherited was, first and foremost, a dream,” says 32-year-old Chang, who helms the family office and has also taken over his father’s role as president of Bangkok University.
Chang recalls having sashimi with his father when the idea for calling the museum Dib, which means “raw” or “authentic” in Thai, came up. Petch had briefly considered naming it after himself, but they both decided against it.
“I want this to be the people’s museum. I didn’t want our family name attached to it,” Chang says. “I would be happy one day if people just walk in and have no idea who founded it.”

More than three decades in the planning, the project is much needed in a country with a shortage of major contemporary art museums.
Stefano Rabolli Pansera, founding director of Khao Yai Art, a non-profit foundation, describes the Thai art scene as “a solar system without a sun until Dib Bangkok opened”, and sees the institution as an important anchor.
Dib adds a large permanent collection of art to a new wave of private art initiatives in the city that includes the Bangkok Art Biennale, launched by ThaiBev and the former permanent secretary of Thailand’s Ministry of Culture, Apinan Poshyananda; Khao Yai Art, founded by the collector Marisa Chearavanont; and the coming deCentral, a social enterprise for art founded by retail and property giant Central Group.
“Bangkok is not like New York, London or Hong Kong, where you have MoMA, Tate, M+ or even Tai Kwun,” Chang says. “There isn’t an institution [with regular programming] here.”

In the absence of government support, Chang says there is currently an “unplanned cultural renaissance” unfolding across the country.
Dib Bangkok also marks a new chapter for the Osathanugrahs, a prominent family in Thailand. Originally Chinese immigrants with the surname Lim, they opened the Bangkok pharmacy Teck Heng Yoo in 1891, producing a popular Chinese stomach remedy.
In 1931, they were bestowed the name Osathanugrah, which means “provider of healing” in Thai, by King Vajiravudh. The business later evolved into Osotspa as it expanded into drinks, healthcare and other products. In 1962, Petch’s father Surat – also a photographer – and mother Pongtip founded Bangkok University.
Miwako Tezuka, director of Dib Bangkok and a former curator at New York’s Asia Society Museum, says that public education is a core mission for the museum, which has a satellite project space called Dib26 nearby for educational and community programmes.
When planning the inaugural exhibition, titled “(In)visible Presence”, the team focused on interactive, multisensory artworks from the collection as they wanted to make the show accessible to a broader audience.
Featuring 81 works by 40 artists, the show includes seminal pieces by Thai artists as well as prominent international names such as Anselm Kiefer and Louise Bourgeois.
Petch had picked some pieces for the museum before he died, such as Thai artist Surasi Kusolwong’s upside-down Volkswagen Beetle suspended from the ceiling, in which visitors can sit.
The heart of the exhibition is a body of installations and sculptures by the late Bangkok-born artist Montien Boonma. Petch was one of the most dedicated collectors of the artist’s works. Alongside iconic pieces like Lotus Sound (1992) – a meditative installation consisting of 500 terracotta bells and gilded wooden lotus leaves – beautiful lesser-known works such as Group of Primary Form (1989), made up of organic sculptures made of hen cages, rice paper and resin, are also on view.

“It’s visual poetry that captures the essence of a lot of Thai culture, heritage, religion and spirituality,” Chang says. “There is a spiritual core or a subtle thread that runs through our collection: art is a window to something deeper and allows for reconciliation. My dad had some things he needed to reconcile, and art was one of the ways he did that.”
Spiritual themes underpin the museum’s architecture. To realise his vision, Petch reportedly changed architects about six times before selecting Kulapat.
Visitors first encounter a serene reflection pool in the vast central courtyard, which sets the tone for the rest of the space. The 7,000 square metre (75,350 sq ft) building, which houses 11 gallery spaces, was inspired by Buddhist enlightenment and unfolds as a journey upwards across three storeys. Among the most striking aspects of the design are a tall conical gallery clad in porcelain tiles called The Chapel and a silo-shaped tower with a steep staircase housing an ethereal permanent installation by James Turrell.
Some pivotal works in the collection influenced the design of the building, such as Thai artist Somboon Hormtientong’s powerful 1995 installation, The Unheard Voice. Consisting of 14 giant Lanna Buddhist temple pillars laid horizontally with rows of delicate lights overhead, the installation is housed in a monastic gallery specially created for the work.

Hormtientong had been storing the pillars on Petch’s behalf for more than two decades, waiting for the museum to be built. “Petch had a special feeling for this work, and he saw it as part of his family,” the artist says.
Many art professionals and Thai artists have affectionate stories about Petch and his support of the local art scene.
“He was building the museum forever. The opening is very sentimental for everyone,” says renowned Thai artist and filmmaker Korakrit Arunanondchai. “I met Petch when I was 16. He was playing guitar on the beach, and I asked to join him, and later he actually became my biggest patron.”
Sukontip “Fon” Nakasem, founder of Warin Lab Contemporary, describes Petch as an educated, focused collector with a generous spirit.
“What sets him apart is that he was passionate about people and invested time and energy in them, which is rare,” she says.
Asked what Petch would do if he were alive to see his dream museum realised, his son Chang replies: “He’d probably be hopping and shouting: ‘Ah… Oh my God, it’s here,’ and he’d probably tear up the way I did when I first saw Somboon Hormtientong’s Unheard Voice [finally] installed.”
